stimulus: Andromeda is a large spiral galaxy with a halo of older stars surrounding its disk. Based on analyses of starlight from about 180,000 halo stars (collected by ground-based observatories and a space telescope), astrophysicists Lien Tran and Otto Hoffmann claim that some halo stars are billions of years older than others — suggesting the halo formed in distinct waves rather than all at once.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the researchers’ claim?

answerOptions.content: The space telescope used by the researchers can also detect stars in nearby dwarf galaxies.
The disk of Andromeda contains roughly twice as many bright stars as its halo does.
There is an age difference of about 3 billion years between certain halo stars in Andromeda.
Halo stars in Andromeda tend to have very similar chemical compositions overall.

rationale:
Choice C is correct because an observed age gap of about 3 billion years between certain halo stars directly supports the claim that some halo stars are billions of years older than others.

Choice A is incorrect: detection capability does not show an age difference. Choice B is incorrect: brightness counts don’t address star ages. Choice D is incorrect: very similar chemical compositions would actually weaken the claim, since stars formed in different waves often have different compositions.
